does: This paper introduces CompassJudger-1, an open-source judge LLM designed to automate subjective evaluation of language models across multiple assessment formats. The model performs unitary scoring, pairwise model comparisons acting as a reward model, generates structured critiques, and executes diverse general tasks. The authors also establish JudgerBench, a comprehensive benchmark dataset spanning various subjective evaluation tasks and topics to provide a unified evaluation framework for assessing judge models. The work addresses the practical bottleneck of expensive and non-reproducible human evaluation by creating a versatile automated judge that maintains alignment with human preferences while enabling efficient model assessment at scale.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of automated LLM evaluation, extending beyond earlier single-task judging approaches that typically focused on either scoring or pairwise comparison alone. It responds to growing recognition that subjective human evaluation, while valuable for capturing real-world preference alignment, severely limits iteration speed in model development. The paper combines insights from reward modeling literature, which demonstrated how models can learn to score outputs, with multi-task learning approaches that enable single models to handle diverse evaluation formats. CompassJudger-1 addresses limitations in prior judge models by combining multiple evaluation capabilities in one unified system rather than requiring separate specialized models for different assessment types.
enables: This work enables faster iteration cycles in LLM development by providing a reproducible, cost-effective alternative to human evaluation that scales to continuous model refinement. The introduction of JudgerBench creates infrastructure for standardized judge model comparison, encouraging development of increasingly capable evaluators. CompassJudger-1's multi-task design opens pathways for more sophisticated evaluation practices, including real-time feedback during training, automated curriculum design based on model weaknesses identified through critique generation, and ensemble judge approaches combining multiple judges. The work also facilitates research into preference learning and alignment by providing accessible tools for large-scale evaluation, potentially enabling future models trained explicitly to optimize for judge-based metrics aligned with human preferences.
